target: Part 6 SHA-256 Recycling section — fix factual errors, expand April batch analysis, restructure for impact
holes_addressed: CRITICAL: Hardcoded count understated April batch restatements (was 8, actual 18 of 33)
MAJOR: crypto-hashed count was 18, actual is 19 in April batch (26 total catalog)
MAJOR: Missing WIN-053 quintuple-restatement — most extreme recycling example
MODERATE: Git timestamp precision — distinguished author-settable vs server-side push
MODERATE: Dome's '9 prospective confirmations' claim not directly addressed
MINOR: Restructured to lead with recycling stats, not crypto competence acknowledgment
new_evidence_added: Dome's own 'What Prospective Means' framework quoted: prospective = timestamped before confirming data was pulled
WIN-053 generates 4 separate PRED-TOROID entries — single observation, one WIN, four 'predictions'
Dome claims 9 WINs as prospective (009, 010, 011, 013, 015, 040, 043, 056, 057) — all 9 fail under our verdicts
April batch: 18/33 restate WINs (55%), 4 more cite data predating registration, leaving 11 novel (33%)

anticipated_objections.objection: The P-51-001 through P-51-010 batch IS genuinely timestamped 48h before the event window — git commit April 3, events April 5-9
anticipated_objections.response: Acknowledged in the text. This batch is the strongest example in the catalog. But 8 of the 10 P-51 predictions test unfalsifiable or non-discriminating outcomes (White Horizon social media reports, Starlink packet loss from Downdetector), limiting the batch's discriminating power even with genuine temporal priority.

anticipated_objections.objection: Some 'restatement' predictions extend prior WINs with more specific numerical ranges (e.g., PRED-095 adds ±2 nT precision to WIN-068)
anticipated_objections.response: Fair point for PRED-095 specifically — noted in text. But the added precision derives from the same κ=1.67 parameter our review disputes (WIN-012). A more precise prediction built on a disputed constant is not independently stronger.

anticipated_objections.objection: Your Section 3.1b standard is so strict that no prediction by anyone with domain knowledge could ever qualify — even Einstein's perihelion calculation was post-hoc
anticipated_objections.response: Addressed explicitly: Einstein's calculation used a THEORY (general relativity) derived from different data (equivalence principle, Newtonian limit) to predict an observation (perihelion advance) in a different domain. The dome's recycled predictions restate the SAME observation under a new ID — that is not analogous.

anticipated_objections.objection: Registration date discrepancy (April 4 vs April 5 commit) is just timezone ambiguity — author may have drafted on April 4 local time
anticipated_objections.response: Possible. Noted as potentially innocent. But the dome's own stated standard is 'git commit timestamp is proof of prior art.' By that standard, the registration date should match the commit date.

kernel_of_truth: The dome author has invested genuine engineering effort in cryptographic provenance infrastructure — SHA-256 hashes, public git history, OpenTimestamps for some WINs. This IS more methodological rigor than most alternative cosmologies attempt. The P-51 batch (April 3 commit, April 5-9 event window) represents genuine temporal priority for that specific subset. Some predictions (PRED-TESLA-003, PRED-TOROID-001, PRED-R005) are genuinely novel and testable. We credit all of this before showing why the aggregate catalog is still overwhelmingly post-hoc.
replacement_html:
SHA-256 Recycling — Crypto Envelopes Around Known Answers

More than a quarter of the dome's predictions catalog restates already-confirmed WINs. Of {{PRED_TOTAL}} catalog entries, {{PRED_RECYCLED}} explicitly restate existing WINs — the same observation appears once on the scorecard and again in the predictions registry, inflating the apparent breadth. The most extreme case: WIN-053 (two-pole geomagnetic model) generates four separate PRED-TOROID entries (002, 004, 005, 007), each repackaging one WIN's observation as a distinct "prediction." Across the full catalog, 12 categories and {{PRED_TOTAL}} entries create an appearance of predictive diversity; the underlying novel content is substantially less.

The crypto infrastructure wrapping these entries is technically competent — which makes the recycling pattern more striking, not less. On April 3–5, 2026, the dome registered 33 new prediction entries in three batches — the largest expansion of the predictions catalog to date. Nineteen carry individual SHA-256 cryptographic hashes committed to the public git repository. This represents genuine engineering effort that most alternative cosmologies never attempt. Credit where due.

But the content inside the crypto envelopes is the problem. Of the 33 April batch entries, 18 (55%) directly restate already-confirmed WINs: 9 from the PRED-0xx series (PRED-094→WIN-001, PRED-099→WIN-065, PRED-100→WIN-056, PRED-090→WIN-066, PRED-091→WIN-066, PRED-082→WIN-054, PRED-097→WIN-048, PRED-095→WIN-068, PRED-072→WIN-042), 3 from PRED-TESLA (001→WIN-062, 002→WIN-001, 004→WIN-002), 5 from PRED-TOROID (002→WIN-053, 003→WIN-038, 004→WIN-053, 005→WIN-053, 007→WIN-053), and PRED-TIER-2→WIN-011. Four more cite data that has been publicly available for years: PRED-084 and PRED-085 describe P-wave and S-wave shadow zones known since the 1930s; PRED-087 references the Indian Ocean geoid low mapped by GRACE since 2002; PRED-111 cites GRACE mascon data published since 2006. That leaves 11 of 33 (33%) with novel content — and even among those, several face the non-discriminating problem: the globe model predicts overlapping ranges for eclipse magnetic effects (PRED-TIER-1), Schumann storm response (PRED-093), and perigee tidal amplification (PRED-078).

Timestamping a known answer with SHA-256 does not retroactively make it prospective. It proves when the author re-registered the claim, not when the underlying observation was first known. Across the 26 crypto-hashed entries in the full catalog, we classify 22 (85%) as post-hoc under our Section 3.1b standard: the confirming data was publicly available before registration, the predicted values appear in standard references, and no independent pre-registration venue corroborates temporal priority beyond the author's own git repository. Only 4 crypto-hashed entries qualify as genuinely prospective and non-recycled — and even these face discriminating-power questions.

The dome's own framework concedes the point. The predictions page defines "prospective" as "timestamped before confirming data was pulled. Not retrofitted." By this standard, the author classifies 9 WINs as prospective confirmations (WIN-009, 010, 011, 013, 015, 040, 043, 056, 057). But under our review's analysis, every one of these 9 carries a verdict of Refuted by Data, Self-Contradicted, Misleading, Not Demonstrated, or Std Model Explains. Being timestamped before data arrival does not help a prediction that fails for other reasons — wrong physics, circular derivation, or standard-model explanability. The temporal priority is real; the predictive content is not.

A note on git timestamps. Git commit timestamps are author-settable — the GIT_AUTHOR_DATE and GIT_COMMITTER_DATE environment variables allow arbitrary dates. The April batch (PRED-072 through PRED-115) claims registration on April 4 but was committed on April 5 at 04:47 EDT, a discrepancy the dome's own methodology should have caught. Only GitHub's server-side push timestamp provides independent verification, and even that proves only when the commit was pushed, not when the prediction was conceived. For genuinely independent temporal anchoring, the dome should use what it already knows how to use: OpenTimestamps blockchain anchoring, which it employed for WINs 035–039 but chose not to apply to the larger April batch. This is significant — the author chose the weaker method for the batch that would most benefit from strong timestamping.

The OTS gap for self-declared prospective WINs. (See Timestamp Error for the full analysis.) The five OTS-anchored WINs (035–039) face a different problem: OTS timestamps status_history.json, which bundles prediction parameters together with observation results in a single file. The hash covers the entire file state at commit time — not an isolated prediction document. To demonstrate temporal priority, the author would need to timestamp a predictions-only document separately from the results document, then show that the prediction hash predates the observation hash. This separation has not been implemented.

The structural argument. The dome author has responded to timestamping critiques by adding more elaborate cryptographic infrastructure. This is, in one sense, how science should work: a critic identifies a weakness, and the model builder strengthens the evidence base. But the upgrade addresses the envelope while leaving the content unchanged. Wrapping a known answer in a stronger cryptographic wrapper makes the wrapper verifiable — it does not make the answer a prediction. The dome's own "What Prospective Means" distinction between prospective and backtested predictions is the right framework. Applied consistently to the dome's own catalog, it shows that the overwhelming majority of entries are, by the author's own definition, backtested.
